title: DEQGAN: Learning the Loss Function for PINNs with Generative Adversarial Networks

reviews.summary: The paper proposes a method to solve ordinary and partial differential equations using GANs. Following in the tradition of physics-informed neural networks (PINNs), the flow equation is parametrized by a neural network. The parameters of the neural network are optimised by replacing a hand-tuned loss function such as the mean-squared error, with a discriminator and an adversarial loss. This method appears to provide improved performance over PINNs trained with hand-tuned loss functions and numerical solvers, on several well-known differential equations.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:
1) The paper is very well-written, well-structured and easy to follow. The method and results, for the most part, are described in clear detail.
2) The ablation study, and discussion of methods to ensure stable training were extremely interesting and relevant, given that GANs are known to be difficult to train.
2) The extensive results in the paper make a convincing case for using GANs over other methods for solving differential equations -- in particular, with respect to the variability of the solutions from other methods across different problems.

Weaknesses:
1) Many of the details of training, hyperparameters etc., included in the methods section (sections 3.2.1, 3.2.2, 3.4), while important for reproducibility, distract from the main message of the paper, and hinder readability.
2) Some of the results require a lot more elucidation: e.g. the details of the ablation study in section 5.2 are mostly relegated to the appendix, and thus make the import of the results hard to parse; the plots in Figure 3 are barely mentioned or described in the text. 
3) The paper could improve with a more extensive discussion of the limitations of the method (the conclusion mentions non-interpretability of the discriminator, but not much more).
4) The paper claims to present two "methods" to improve robustness in training: residual monitoring and addition of instance noise. While it is important to document the techniques used to stabilised training, neither of these are nescessarily novel contributions in the context of GANs. Furthermore, it is not clear whether these measures would be equally effective without spectral normalisation, skip connections in the generator, or with different choice of variance for the instance noise, or a change in the 25% training iteration threshold for residual monitoring.
reviews.questions:
As outlined in the previous section, I believe the paper makes a strong case for using GANs to solve differential equations. Some concrete suggestions to address the weaknesses:
1) It would be good to move the details of sections 3.2.1, 3.2.2 and 3.4 to the appendix, and move details of the ablation study to the main paper. Furthermore, sections 3.3 and 4.1 describe training / engineering choices to a level of detail that hinders readability, and can certainly be condensed.
2) It would be good to have a more extensive discussion of Figure 3. Moreover, Figure 3 appears to show only simulations from the GAN -- it would be good to have some examples in of the simulations from other methods as well, in order to contextualize the GAN performance beyond the reported MSE numbers.
3) While the methods residual monitoring and instance noise are certainly important details for reproducibility, it is not convincing that they would work without the other hyperparameter or architectural choices. It would therefore be good to modify the discussion of these two "methods", and to present them as potential avenues for stable training rather than a contribution of the paper.

Questions:
1) The performance of the different methods for solving differential equations are compared solely on the basis of MSE. Is there a particular reason for choosing this metric? -- it is particularly surprising that there is no justification provided for this in the paper, since the introduction makes a strong point that the choice of similarity metric typically used to optimize parameters for the PINNs are arbitrary. It would also be good to see whether the GAN performance is equally good under other similarity metrics e.g. L1, or MMD 
2) In line 181-182, "We use the ReLU function...noise should not be used." -- this logic is not clear. Why should $L_g < L_d$ on any particular iteration indicate a better generator? -- this could also be due to initialisation, especially at the beginning of training.
3) How does the coarseness of the grid from which $t, x$ are sampled affect the performance of the GANs over other methods?

Minor comments:
1) The notation in section 4 is confusing in places: $G(x)$ and $\Psi_{\theta}(x)$ are used somewhat interchangeably; in line 179-181 $L_g$ and $L_d$ are not defined.
2) In Figure 2, a legend is not required in every panel
3) In Figure 3, the dotted lines in (a) and (b) are note identified in the legend; the colourbars have no label in (c) and (d)
reviews.limitations:
It would be good if the discussion could address the following issues:
1) While the discriminator is not interpretable, the generator has potentially the same issues. What scientific insight is gained from an (effectively) black box generator modeling a flow equation?
2) While the GANs do provide an improvement over existing methods in terms of performance, it would be good to have an idea of the associated computational costs, sample efficiency and training times compared to the other methods.
3) How does this method scale with the number of dynamical variables, the size of the coarseness of the $t, x$ grid?
reviews.rating: 7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.

Rating:
7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
Summary
This paper proposes a GAN-based framework that learns a task-specific loss for solving differential equations, and demonstrates strong performance across a set of ODE/PDE benchmarks. The method is clearly presented, with an ablation and a set of stability techniques tailored to the challenges of GAN training in this setting. Empirically, the approach outperforms PINNs using hand-tuned losses and baseline numerical solvers, and highlights the variability of existing methods across problems. Overall, the paper is technically solid and promising.

Strengths
- Clear, well-structured presentation; the core method and experimental setup are easy to follow.
- The ablation and stability techniques (e.g., instance noise, residual monitoring) directly address known GAN training issues in scientific problems and are practically useful.
- Extensive results convincingly show improved accuracy over PINNs with fixed losses and numerical solvers on multiple benchmarks; the comparisons illustrate the brittleness of hand-tuned losses across tasks.

Weaknesses and points needing clarification
- Readability: The main text includes substantial training and hyperparameter detail (notably Sections 3.2.1, 3.2.2, 3.4) that distracts from the main narrative. Sections 3.3 and 4.1 also read as implementation-heavy and could be condensed.
- Under-discussed results: Key ablation details (Section 5.2) are mostly relegated to the appendix, and Figure 3 is barely analyzed in the main text; moreover, Figure 3 appears to show only GAN simulations, limiting comparative context.
- Limited discussion of limitations beyond the non-interpretability of the discriminator.
- Novelty and independence of stability techniques: Residual monitoring and instance noise are standard in the GAN literature; it is unclear whether their effectiveness here depends on architectural and hyperparameter choices (e.g., spectral normalization, skip connections), the choice of instance noise variance, or the specific 25% residual-monitoring threshold.
- Evaluation metric: The paper relies exclusively on MSE. Given the critique of arbitrary loss selection in PINNs, this choice needs justification and complementary metrics.
- Instance noise scheduling: The schedule based on ReLU(Lg − Ld) needs a clearer rationale. Why should Lg < Ld imply less noise? Could early behavior be dominated by initialization rather than relative progress of G and D?
- Sampling/grid effects: The impact of (t, x) grid coarseness on the GAN’s performance, and how this compares to other methods, is not discussed.
- Presentation issues: Inconsistent notation (G(x) vs. Ψθ(x)), undefined Lg and Ld at first use; figure polish (redundant legends in Fig. 2; unidentified dotted lines in Fig. 3a–b; unlabeled colorbars in Fig. 3c–d).

Suggestions for improvement
- Streamline the main text by moving detailed training and hyperparameter material (Sections 3.2.1, 3.2.2, 3.4) to the appendix; condense Sections 3.3 and 4.1.
- Elevate ablation content from the appendix into the main paper and substantially expand the discussion of Figure 3. Where possible, include comparative simulations from alternative methods to contextualize performance beyond MSE plots.
- Reframe residual monitoring and instance noise as practical stability aids rather than core methodological contributions, and discuss their sensitivity. Specifically:
  - Analyze dependence on architectural choices (e.g., spectral normalization, skip connections).
  - Report sensitivity to the instance noise variance and to the 25% residual-monitoring threshold.
- Justify the use of MSE given the paper’s critique of fixed losses, and report additional metrics (e.g., L1, MMD) to assess robustness of conclusions.
- Clarify the instance noise schedule:
  - Define Lg and Ld at first mention and explain the assumption behind ReLU(Lg − Ld).
  - Provide evidence that the schedule reflects discriminator/generator balance rather than initialization artifacts.
- Discuss how the coarseness of the (t, x) sampling grid affects performance relative to baselines.
- Expand the limitations section to address:
  - Scientific insight obtainable from a black-box generator;
  - Computational cost, sample efficiency, and training time versus baselines;
  - Scalability with the number of variables and grid resolution.
- Fix notation and figure issues:
  - Use consistent symbols (avoid interchanging G(x) and Ψθ(x)).
  - Define Lg and Ld on first use.
  - Remove redundant legends (Fig. 2), identify dotted lines (Fig. 3a–b), and label colorbars (Fig. 3c–d).

Overall assessment
This is a well-executed and timely paper that advances data-driven PDE/ODE solution methods by learning a loss via a GAN framework, with compelling empirical gains over PINNs and numerical solvers. While the paper would benefit from improved readability, deeper discussion of ablations, clearer positioning of stability tricks, broader evaluation metrics, and a more thorough limitations analysis, these are addressable and do not undermine the core contribution.

Rating: 7 — Accept.

Questions:
As outlined in the previous section, I believe the paper makes a strong case for using GANs to solve differential equations. Some concrete suggestions to address the weaknesses:
1) It would be good to move the details of sections 3.2.1, 3.2.2 and 3.4 to the appendix, and move details of the ablation study to the main paper. Furthermore, sections 3.3 and 4.1 describe training / engineering choices to a level of detail that hinders readability, and can certainly be condensed.
2) It would be good to have a more extensive discussion of Figure 3. Moreover, Figure 3 appears to show only simulations from the GAN -- it would be good to have some examples in of the simulations from other methods as well, in order to contextualize the GAN performance beyond the reported MSE numbers.
3) While the methods residual monitoring and instance noise are certainly important details for reproducibility, it is not convincing that they would work without the other hyperparameter or architectural choices. It would therefore be good to modify the discussion of these two "methods", and to present them as potential avenues for stable training rather than a contribution of the paper.

Questions:
1) The performance of the different methods for solving differential equations are compared solely on the basis of MSE. Is there a particular reason for choosing this metric? -- it is particularly surprising that there is no justification provided for this in the paper, since the introduction makes a strong point that the choice of similarity metric typically used to optimize parameters for the PINNs are arbitrary. It would also be good to see whether the GAN performance is equally good under other similarity metrics e.g. L1, or MMD 
2) In line 181-182, "We use the ReLU function...noise should not be used." -- this logic is not clear. Why should $L_g < L_d$ on any particular iteration indicate a better generator? -- this could also be due to initialisation, especially at the beginning of training.
3) How does the coarseness of the grid from which $t, x$ are sampled affect the performance of the GANs over other methods?

Minor comments:
1) The notation in section 4 is confusing in places: $G(x)$ and $\Psi_{\theta}(x)$ are used somewhat interchangeably; in line 179-181 $L_g$ and $L_d$ are not defined.
2) In Figure 2, a legend is not required in every panel
3) In Figure 3, the dotted lines in (a) and (b) are note identified in the legend; the colourbars have no label in (c) and (d)

Limitations:
It would be good if the discussion could address the following issues:
1) While the discriminator is not interpretable, the generator has potentially the same issues. What scientific insight is gained from an (effectively) black box generator modeling a flow equation?
2) While the GANs do provide an improvement over existing methods in terms of performance, it would be good to have an idea of the associated computational costs, sample efficiency and training times compared to the other methods.
3) How does this method scale with the number of dynamical variables, the size of the coarseness of the $t, x$ grid?

Rating:
7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.

extracted_ideas.summary: The review is positive, praising a well-written paper that proposes using GANs to learn a loss for solving differential equations and showing strong performance over PINNs and numerical solvers. However, the reviewer flags readability concerns, insufficient exposition of the ablation/stability results, limited discussion of limitations, and questions the novelty and independence of the robustness techniques.
strengths: Well-written, well-structured, and easy to follow; method and results are clearly described.
Ablation study and stability techniques are interesting and highly relevant given GAN training challenges.
Extensive results convincingly demonstrate improved performance over PINNs with hand-tuned losses and numerical solvers, highlighting variability of other methods across problems.
weaknesses: Excessive training and hyperparameter details in the main text (e.g., sections 3.2.1, 3.2.2, 3.4) distract from the main message and reduce readability.
Some results are under-explained; ablation details are mostly in the appendix and Figure 3 is barely discussed in the main text.
Limited discussion of method limitations beyond the non-interpretability of the discriminator.

Residual monitoring and instance noise are presented as contributions despite not being novel in GANs; unclear if their effectiveness holds without specific architectural and hyperparameter choices (e.g., spectral normalization, skip connections, instance noise variance, 25% residual monitoring threshold).
suggestions: Move detailed training and hyperparameter content (sections 3.2.1, 3.2.2, 3.4) to the appendix and bring ablation study details into the main paper; condense sections 3.3 and 4.1.
Provide a more extensive discussion of Figure 3 and include comparative simulations from other methods to contextualize GAN performance beyond MSE.
Reframe residual monitoring and instance noise as practical stability aids rather than core methodological contributions.
Justify the choice of MSE as the evaluation metric and report performance under additional metrics (e.g., L1, MMD).
Clarify the rationale for the instance noise schedule using ReLU(Lg − Ld) and why Lg < Ld implies less noise; address the possibility of effects due to initialization.
Discuss how grid coarseness for sampling (t, x) affects GAN performance relative to other methods.
Address broader limitations: scientific insight from a black-box generator, computational cost/sample efficiency/training time vs. baselines, and scaling with number of variables and grid resolution.
Fix notation and figure presentation issues: avoid using G(x) and Ψθ(x) interchangeably; define Lg and Ld; remove redundant legends; identify dotted lines; label colorbars.
main_points: The GAN-based approach appears effective and outperforms PINNs with fixed losses across several benchmark ODEs/PDEs.
Readability is hindered by overly detailed training/hyperparameter content in the main text; key ablation insights are insufficiently discussed there.
The stability techniques (instance noise, residual monitoring) are not novel and may be contingent on specific architectural/hyperparameter choices.
The evaluation relies solely on MSE without justification, which is inconsistent with the paper’s critique of arbitrary loss choices in PINNs.
The logic behind the instance noise scheduling based on Lg and Ld needs clarification.
The effect of grid coarseness on performance is not addressed.
Limitations section needs deepening (interpretability, computational costs, scalability).
Minor notation and figure clarity issues reduce polish.
technical_details: Sections 3.2.1, 3.2.2, and 3.4 contain detailed training and hyperparameter information that could be moved to the appendix.
Sections 3.3 and 4.1 include training/engineering choices that can be condensed.
Ablation study details (section 5.2) are mostly in the appendix; Figure 3 is insufficiently discussed in the main text and appears to show only GAN simulations.

Concerns about the novelty and independence of residual monitoring and instance noise from architectural choices like spectral normalization and skip connections, as well as sensitivity to instance noise variance and the 25% residual monitoring threshold.
Evaluation metric: only MSE is used; reviewer requests justification and additional metrics (e.g., L1, MMD).

Instance noise schedule uses ReLU(Lg − Ld); reviewer questions the assumption that Lg < Ld implies the discriminator is performing worse or that less noise should be used; initialization effects may confound this.
Impact of the coarseness of the sampling grid for (t, x) on GAN performance is not discussed.
Notation issues: G(x) and Ψθ(x) used interchangeably; Lg and Ld not defined at first mention.
Figure issues: redundant legends in Figure 2; unidentified dotted lines in Figure 3 panels (a) and (b); unlabeled colorbars in Figure 3 panels (c) and (d).

reviews.text:
Summary
This paper proposes a novel GAN-based framework that “learns” the loss function for physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) in an unsupervised manner, i.e., without access to solution data. The approach is supported by a careful integration of stabilizing techniques for adversarial training and is evaluated extensively, showing multiple orders of magnitude improvements on a variety of differential equation tasks. The paper is clearly written, and the experiments are presented in a transparent way with substantial detail in the appendix. I recommend acceptance.

Strengths
- Clear novelty: reframing PINN training via a GAN that learns the loss function without solution supervision is an original and compelling idea.
- Strong empirical results: the method achieves substantial gains—multiple orders of magnitude—across differential equation benchmarks.
- Practical robustness: the authors incorporate effective techniques for stabilizing GAN training (as summarized in Section 3), which appears instrumental to the reported performance.
- Presentation and reproducibility: the exposition is strong, and the appendix provides extensive experimental details that enhance transparency.

Weaknesses
- Lack of discussion on mode collapse: given the adversarial setup, it is important to consider whether the generator could converge to different valid (or spurious) solutions of the same equation and under what conditions such collapse or multiplicity might occur.
- Absence of theoretical analysis: the paper would benefit from theoretical insights or guarantees to complement the empirical evidence.

Suggestions for Improvement
- Include a focused discussion on mode collapse: clarify how the proposed training dynamics mitigate collapse, whether there are diagnostics to detect it, and how it might affect solution diversity or correctness in practice.
- Provide an ablation study on the stabilization techniques: quantify the contribution of the individual “tricks” used for stable GAN training to establish their necessity and impact on performance and stability.

Additional Comments
- The limitations are acknowledged in the conclusion, and I agree there is no apparent negative societal impact arising from this work as presented.

Overall Assessment
This is a technically solid and innovative contribution with strong empirical validation and good presentation. While the lack of theoretical analysis and the missing discussion of mode collapse are notable gaps, they do not outweigh the clear advances demonstrated here. Addressing the requested discussion and ablation would further strengthen the paper.

Recommendation: Accept (7)

reviews.summary: This work proposes Differential Equation GAN (DEQGAN) for solving differential equations using generative adversarial networks (GAN) where the generator learns to output the solution and the discriminator tries to learn a good loss function. The authors compare DEQGAN against classical PINNs over twelve ordinary differential equations (ODEs) and partial differential equations (PDEs). The experimental results show that DEQGAN can achieve multiple orders of magnitude lower L2 error than PINNs.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths: the paper is well-written and easy to follow. DEQGAN demonstrates superior empirical results compared to classical PINNs. The fact that DEQGAN doesn't depend on predefined distance such as L1, L2 can be useful in some problems.

Weakness:
1. The Related Work section misses some important discussion [1, 2]. The authors should discuss the relationship to works and place the contribution of DEQGAN in the context of prior works. For example, SA-PINNs [1] can also be viewed as learning a loss function.
2. The authors state that the L2 loss lacks theoretical justification but miss the important explanation on why and when L2 loss can be problematic. Furthermore, DEQGAN do not have a theoretical justification of the learned loss function either.
3. The loss function in DEQGAN is borrowed from generative adversarial network (GAN). The original GAN work designs its loss function such that generator is minimizing Jensen-Shannon divergence between the generated distribution and target distribution given optimal discriminator dynamics. However, the Jensen-Shannon divergence doesn't make sense in DEQGAN because the problem is to solve a differential equation with unique solution. Even with Gaussian instance noise, minimizing the divergence between two Gaussians will just be minimizing the L2 loss. I do not see why a generative model is needed here.
4. The equations considered in the experiments are artificial. Only one specific initial condition is used for each equation, which raise the concern that the authors may pick the initial conditions to put DEQGAN in advantage in the experimental results.
5. DEQGAN has training instability issue. To solve one equation, it needs to repeat runs multiple times and filters out the ones with poor performances.

[1] McClenny, Levi, and Ulisses Braga-Neto. "Self-adaptive physics-informed neural networks using a soft attention mechanism." arXiv preprint arXiv:2009.04544 (2020).
[2] Daw, Arka, M. Maruf, and Anuj Karpatne. "PID-GAN: A GAN Framework based on a Physics-informed Discriminator for Uncertainty Quantification with Physics." Proceedings of the 27th ACM SIGKDD Conference on Knowledge Discovery & Data Mining. 2021.
reviews.questions:
1. The authors motivate the need of learning loss function by saying that L2,L1 loss functions lack theoretical justification. However, the author challenges the standard practice in physics-informed learning without explaining why and when L1, L2 loss functions can be bad choices. If L2 loss is problematic, how is the learned loss function better than L2 loss? Can the authors provide theoretical justification for it?
2. How fast is DEQGAN compared to PINNs and numerical solvers? Can authors provide time complexity comparison against PINNs and its improved variants such as SA-PINNs?
3. Only one specific artificial initial condition is used for each equation. How will DEQGAN perform if the initial condition is a random function sampled from a random field?
reviews.limitations:
1. Lack of theoretical justification of the loss function and how the disciminator can learn a better objective function.
2. DEQGAN has training instability issue. To solve one equation, it needs to repeat runs multiple times and filters out the ones with poor performances.
3. The equations considered in the experiments are artificial. Only one specific initial condition is used for each equation, which raise the concern that the authors may pick the initial conditions to put DEQGAN in advantage in the experimental results.
reviews.rating: 3: Reject: For instance, a paper with technical flaws, weak evaluation, inadequate reproducibility and incompletely addressed ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 5: You are absolutely certain about your assessment. You are very familiar with the related work and checked the math/other details carefully.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This work proposes Differential Equation GAN (DEQGAN) for solving differential equations using generative adversarial networks (GAN) where the generator learns to output the solution and the discriminator tries to learn a good loss function. The authors compare DEQGAN against classical PINNs over twelve ordinary differential equations (ODEs) and partial differential equations (PDEs). The experimental results show that DEQGAN can achieve multiple orders of magnitude lower L2 error than PINNs.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths: the paper is well-written and easy to follow. DEQGAN demonstrates superior empirical results compared to classical PINNs. The fact that DEQGAN doesn't depend on predefined distance such as L1, L2 can be useful in some problems.

Weakness:
1. The Related Work section misses some important discussion [1, 2]. The authors should discuss the relationship to works and place the contribution of DEQGAN in the context of prior works. For example, SA-PINNs [1] can also be viewed as learning a loss function.
2. The authors state that the L2 loss lacks theoretical justification but miss the important explanation on why and when L2 loss can be problematic. Furthermore, DEQGAN do not have a theoretical justification of the learned loss function either.
3. The loss function in DEQGAN is borrowed from generative adversarial network (GAN). The original GAN work designs its loss function such that generator is minimizing Jensen-Shannon divergence between the generated distribution and target distribution given optimal discriminator dynamics. However, the Jensen-Shannon divergence doesn't make sense in DEQGAN because the problem is to solve a differential equation with unique solution. Even with Gaussian instance noise, minimizing the divergence between two Gaussians will just be minimizing the L2 loss. I do not see why a generative model is needed here.
4. The equations considered in the experiments are artificial. Only one specific initial condition is used for each equation, which raise the concern that the authors may pick the initial conditions to put DEQGAN in advantage in the experimental results.
5. DEQGAN has training instability issue. To solve one equation, it needs to repeat runs multiple times and filters out the ones with poor performances.

[1] McClenny, Levi, and Ulisses Braga-Neto. "Self-adaptive physics-informed neural networks using a soft attention mechanism." arXiv preprint arXiv:2009.04544 (2020).
[2] Daw, Arka, M. Maruf, and Anuj Karpatne. "PID-GAN: A GAN Framework based on a Physics-informed Discriminator for Uncertainty Quantification with Physics." Proceedings of the 27th ACM SIGKDD Conference on Knowledge Discovery & Data Mining. 2021.

Questions:
1. The authors motivate the need of learning loss function by saying that L2,L1 loss functions lack theoretical justification. However, the author challenges the standard practice in physics-informed learning without explaining why and when L1, L2 loss functions can be bad choices. If L2 loss is problematic, how is the learned loss function better than L2 loss? Can the authors provide theoretical justification for it?
2. How fast is DEQGAN compared to PINNs and numerical solvers? Can authors provide time complexity comparison against PINNs and its improved variants such as SA-PINNs?
3. Only one specific artificial initial condition is used for each equation. How will DEQGAN perform if the initial condition is a random function sampled from a random field?

Limitations:
1. Lack of theoretical justification of the loss function and how the disciminator can learn a better objective function.
2. DEQGAN has training instability issue. To solve one equation, it needs to repeat runs multiple times and filters out the ones with poor performances.
3. The equations considered in the experiments are artificial. Only one specific initial condition is used for each equation, which raise the concern that the authors may pick the initial conditions to put DEQGAN in advantage in the experimental results.

Rating:
3: Reject: For instance, a paper with technical flaws, weak evaluation, inadequate reproducibility and incompletely addressed ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
Summary
The paper presents DEQGAN, a GAN-based framework for solving differential equations. The manuscript is clearly written and easy to follow. Empirically, DEQGAN outperforms classical PINNs on the reported test problems and benefits from not committing to a fixed pre-defined discrepancy such as L1 or L2. However, the work lacks theoretical justification for both its critique of standard losses and for the learned discriminator-based objective, omits key related work, employs limited and artificial experimental settings, and exhibits training instability that requires repeated runs and post-hoc filtering. These issues hinder confidence in the generality and soundness of the approach.

Strengths
- The paper is well-structured and readable.
- Demonstrates superior empirical performance over classical PINNs on the presented equations.
- Avoids reliance on fixed distance choices (e.g., L1/L2), which can be advantageous for some problems.

Weaknesses
- Related work is incomplete. Important prior methods are not discussed, including SA-PINNs (McClenny and Braga-Neto, 2020), which can be viewed as learning a loss via soft attention, and PID-GAN (Daw et al., KDD 2021), which uses a physics-informed discriminator. The omission makes it difficult to understand how DEQGAN differs from or improves upon existing approaches.
- The theoretical rationale is underdeveloped. The paper asserts that L2 lacks theoretical justification but does not articulate when and why L1/L2 are inadequate in PINNs. At the same time, there is no clear justification for why the discriminator-learned loss in DEQGAN is principled or preferable.
- The GAN objective interpretation is misaligned with deterministic DEs that typically have unique solutions. Framing the objective via Jensen–Shannon divergence is questionable in this context, and with Gaussian instance noise, minimizing divergence between Gaussians effectively reduces to an L2 criterion, calling into question the necessity of a generative modeling setup.
- Experimental design is narrow. The equations are largely artificial, and only one specific initial condition is used per equation. This raises concerns about potential cherry-picking and limits claims about robustness and generality.
- Training instability is a significant practical issue. Achieving a good solution requires multiple runs with poor-performing runs filtered out, which undermines reliability and reproducibility.
- Efficiency analysis is missing. There are no runtime or complexity comparisons with classical PINNs, stronger PINN variants (e.g., SA-PINNs), or standard numerical solvers.

Suggestions for Improvement
- Expand and deepen the Related Work section to include SA-PINNs and PID-GAN, clarifying conceptual and practical differences. Specify what DEQGAN contributes beyond these methods.
- Provide a precise account of when and why L1/L2 losses fail in PINNs, and offer theoretical justification for the learned discriminator loss. If the method relies on a divergence such as JSD, reconcile this with the uniqueness of solutions in deterministic DEs, and address the reduction to L2 under Gaussian noise.
- Include comprehensive efficiency comparisons: wall-clock runtime, iteration counts, and complexity versus classical PINNs, improved variants (e.g., SA-PINNs), and numerical solvers.
- Broaden the experimental evaluation to include diverse and randomized initial conditions (e.g., sampling initial functions from a random field) to test robustness and mitigate concerns about cherry-picking.
- Address training instability by proposing and evaluating techniques that reduce reliance on multiple restarts and post-hoc filtering.

Decision
Rating: 3 — Reject. While the empirical results versus classical PINNs are promising and the paper is well-written, the lack of theoretical grounding, omission of key related work, limited and potentially biased experimental setup, training instability, and absence of efficiency analysis collectively prevent acceptance at this time.

extracted_ideas.summary: The review acknowledges that DEQGAN is well-written and achieves strong empirical performance versus classical PINNs, but criticizes the work for lacking theoretical justification, missing key related work, employing artificial/limited experimental setups, and relying on unstable training that requires multiple runs and filtering.
strengths: Well-written and easy to follow.
Demonstrates superior empirical results compared to classical PINNs on the tested equations.
Does not depend on predefined distances (e.g., L1, L2), which can be useful for some problems.
weaknesses: Related Work omits important prior work and insufficiently situates DEQGAN in context (e.g., SA-PINNs and PID-GAN).
Claims that L2 lacks theoretical justification without explaining why/when L1/L2 are problematic; DEQGAN’s learned loss also lacks theoretical justification.

The GAN-based loss rationale (Jensen–Shannon divergence) is ill-suited for differential equations with unique solutions; with Gaussian instance noise, minimizing divergence between Gaussians reduces to L2, questioning the need for a generative model.
Experimental equations are artificial and use only one specific initial condition per equation, raising concerns about potential cherry-picking of initial conditions favoring DEQGAN.
Training instability: solving one equation requires multiple runs with poor runs filtered out.
suggestions: Expand Related Work to discuss and compare with SA-PINNs and PID-GAN; clarify how DEQGAN differs and what it contributes beyond these methods.
Provide a clear explanation of when and why L1/L2 losses are inadequate in PINNs, and offer theoretical justification for DEQGAN’s learned loss.
Justify the use of a GAN objective for deterministic DE problems (address the mismatch with Jensen–Shannon divergence and uniqueness of solutions).
Report runtime/time-complexity comparisons against classical PINNs, improved variants (e.g., SA-PINNs), and numerical solvers.
Evaluate robustness across diverse and randomized initial conditions (e.g., initial functions sampled from a random field), not just one specific initial condition per equation.
Address training instability and reduce reliance on multiple repeated runs and post-hoc filtering.
main_points: Theoretical justification is lacking both for dismissing L1/L2 losses and for the discriminator-learned loss in DEQGAN.
The GAN loss interpretation (Jensen–Shannon divergence) does not align with deterministic DEs having unique solutions; with Gaussian noise it effectively reduces to L2.
Key related works (SA-PINNs and PID-GAN) are missing from the discussion; SA-PINNs can also be viewed as learning a loss function.
Experimental validation relies on artificial problems and single initial conditions, limiting generality and raising fairness concerns.
DEQGAN exhibits training instability and requires multiple runs with filtering to obtain good results.
Missing efficiency analysis: no comparison of speed/time complexity vs PINNs, SA-PINNs, or numerical solvers.
technical_details: Reference to SA-PINNs: McClenny and Braga-Neto (2020), which can be viewed as learning a loss function via a soft attention mechanism.
Reference to PID-GAN: Daw et al. (KDD 2021), a GAN framework with a physics-informed discriminator.
Critique of Jensen–Shannon divergence as a GAN objective for DEs with unique solutions.
Claim that with Gaussian instance noise, minimizing divergence between two Gaussians effectively reduces to minimizing L2 loss.
Observation that experiments use only one initial condition per equation and focus on artificial equations.
Training requires repeated runs and filtering of poor-performing runs to obtain good solutions.

reviews.original_rating: 3: Reject: For instance, a paper with technical flaws, weak evaluation, inadequate reproducibility and incompletely addressed ethical considerations.

title: ISAAC Newton: Input-based Approximate Curvature for Newton's Method

reviews.summary: The paper addresses the problem of using second-order information in large-scale machine learning tasks (e.g. training neural networks). It proposes a Kronecker-factored Approximate Curvature (KFAC)-approximated, Tikhonov regularized Gauss-Newton (GN) update step. Instead of a single regularization parameter ($\lambda$), the update step in the paper uses two independent regularization parameters ($\lambda_g$ and $\lambda_a$). The paper relates different settings of $\lambda_a$ and $\lambda_g$ to other algorithms (i.e. classical GN and gradient descent). It shows that allowing $\lambda_g$ grows to infinity gives a computationally efficient update step. It provides empirical results on their proposed method.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:
1. The update step $\xi^*$ is interesting and practical. The paper provides experiments to show that using $\xi^*$ can lead to lower computation time without sacrificing performance. Experiments are thorough and tested on some fairly large neural network architectures.
2. The update step $\xi$ is a nice, compact way to describe an algorithm for training neural networks. The analysis of $\xi$ provides various ways of interpreting the algorithm in terms of different regularization parameter settings. The properties of $\xi$ are interesting. The analysis is relatively thorough (e.g. showing that any combination of $\lambda_g$ and $\lambda_a$ will still result in a descent direction).
3. Paper is well-written and clear. 

Weaknesses
1. Some of the experiments are not benchmarked to SOTA (which is acknowledged by the paper). This might affect people's willingness to adopt the method in practice.

1. Line 70 refers to $H$ in Equation 6. Typo?
2. Is there an intuitive interpretation of the preconditioner $M$ in Remark 2? Does this preconditioning help in terms of reducing the number of iterations needed to reach a solution?

Instead of a single regularization parameter ($\lambda$), the update step in the paper uses two independent regularization parameters ($\lambda_g$ and $\lambda_a$). The paper relates different settings of $\lambda_a$ and $\lambda_g$ to other algorithms (i.e. classical GN and gradient descent). It shows that allowing $\lambda_g$ grows to infinity gives a computationally efficient update step. It provides empirical results on their proposed method.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:
1. The update step $\xi^*$ is interesting and practical. The paper provides experiments to show that using $\xi^*$ can lead to lower computation time without sacrificing performance. Experiments are thorough and tested on some fairly large neural network architectures.
2. The update step $\xi$ is a nice, compact way to describe an algorithm for training neural networks. The analysis of $\xi$ provides various ways of interpreting the algorithm in terms of different regularization parameter settings. The properties of $\xi$ are interesting. The analysis is relatively thorough (e.g. showing that any combination of $\lambda_g$ and $\lambda_a$ will still result in a descent direction).
3. Paper is well-written and clear. 

Weaknesses

reviews.text:
Summary
This paper proposes a K-FAC–approximated, Tikhonov-regularized Gauss-Newton training scheme with two independent regularization parameters (λg and λa). A key result is that letting λg tend to infinity yields an efficient update step (xi*) that substantially reduces computation without degrading performance. The authors provide theoretical guarantees, including that any choice of λg and λa produces a descent direction, and validate the approach empirically on fairly large neural architectures.

Strengths
- The efficient update step (xi*) is interesting and practical, delivering lower computation time without sacrificing accuracy.
- The formulation of the general update (xi) is compact and clear, and it facilitates interpretation across different regularization parameter settings.
- Theoretical analysis is relatively thorough, notably the guarantee that any combination of λg and λa yields a descent direction.
- Experiments are thorough and include fairly large models, supporting the practical viability of the method.
- The paper is well-written and easy to follow.

Weaknesses
- Some experiments are not benchmarked against state-of-the-art baselines, which may limit confidence in practical adoption.

Suggestions for improvement
- Include benchmarking against strong SOTA baselines to strengthen claims of practical relevance and facilitate adoption.
- Clarify whether there is a typo referring to H in Equation 6 (Line 70).
- Provide an intuitive interpretation of the preconditioner M in Remark 2 and discuss whether this preconditioning reduces the number of iterations needed to reach a given solution quality.

Overall assessment
This is a technically solid and well-presented contribution that combines a principled second-order update with a computationally efficient approximation, supported by solid theory and convincing experiments on sizable architectures. While broader SOTA comparisons would further strengthen the empirical case and aid adoption, the current results are compelling. I recommend acceptance.

Rating: 7 — Accept.

reviews.summary:
This paper proposed a KFAC variant to improve the computation efficiency. It introduces two Tikhonov regularization terms into KFAC, and makes gradient conditioning more efficient by force $\lambda_g$ go to infinity. The authors present a theoretical guarantee that the optimization still goes in the right direction even with such an adaptation. The authors also show ISAAC is more computation efficient in small-batch stochastic regimes.

However, I feel contributions in this paper are rather limited, and some motivations might need further justification.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strength:

The paper is well written. Preliminaries and methods are clearly explained. 

Weaknesses:

1, Contributions are limited. Reducing the compute costs of KFAC is a good problem. However, I am not convinced that separating the regularization term and using a large regularization is a novel and effective solution. It only makes the optimizer behave more and more like SGD. As the authors stated in Theorem 1, small $\lambda_a, \lambda_g$ leads to KFAC, while large $\lambda_a, \lambda_g$ leads to vanilla gradient descent. 

2, Empirical results need to be enhanced. The authors use several empirical results to support the claim that ISAAC still behaves like KFAC even though using a strong regularization factor. However, I do not think current results are strong enough to support the claim. The issues in the empirical evaluations also make me doubt the contributions in the paper. 

- 2.1, The author should provide more baselines (Adam, SGD with momentum) so that we can really have a fair evaluation. 

- 2.2, For Figure 1, my understanding is that the authors are trying to show ISAAC still obtains good results (loss and accuracy) even with large regularization. If so, I do not think it is necessary. As the authors stated in Theorem 1, larger regularization makes ISAAC behave more like SGD. Therefore, getting similar loss and accuracy is no surprise.
    
- 2.3, For Figure 2, there are several issues. First, hyper parameters are not fully revealed. For instance, what is the weight decay for each optimizer since weight decays large affects training convergence?  Second, only the training curve is reported. The author really should present both a training and validation curve. In many cases, we can have faster convergence on training dataset, but not for validation dataset. 

- 2.4, For Figure 6, the reported accuracy is obviously lower compared to results we usually have on CIFAR-10. The reason might be that the authors use SGD without momentum. However, I am not convinced under such a setting. SGD with momentum is a default setting in many training tasks. Without momentum, I do not think the results will provide meaningful comparisons and conclusions.

3, Training with small batches should be further justified. I understand the authors stress the compute benefits of ISAAC in a small-batch regime (Theorem 3-ii). However, considering more general settings, the authors should well justify it: is it worth accelerating the training using small batches given the fact that large batches can also improve convergence performance even using SGD. Therefore, I think not only should the author provide results in small-batch regimes, training using large batches should also be evaluated and compared with other baselines.
reviews.questions: See my comments above.
reviews.limitations: No
reviews.rating: 5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

Summary:
This paper proposed a KFAC variant to improve the computation efficiency. It introduces two Tikhonov regularization terms into KFAC, and makes gradient conditioning more efficient by force $\lambda_g$ go to infinity. The authors present a theoretical guarantee that the optimization still goes in the right direction even with such an adaptation. The authors also show ISAAC is more computation efficient in small-batch stochastic regimes.

However, I feel contributions in this paper are rather limited, and some motivations might need further justification.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strength:

The paper is well written. Preliminaries and methods are clearly explained. 

Weaknesses:

1, Contributions are limited. Reducing the compute costs of KFAC is a good problem. However, I am not convinced that separating the regularization term and using a large regularization is a novel and effective solution. It only makes the optimizer behave more and more like SGD. As the authors stated in Theorem 1, small $\lambda_a, \lambda_g$ leads to KFAC, while large $\lambda_a, \lambda_g$ leads to vanilla gradient descent. 

2, Empirical results need to be enhanced. The authors use several empirical results to support the claim that ISAAC still behaves like KFAC even though using a strong regularization factor. However, I do not think current results are strong enough to support the claim. The issues in the empirical evaluations also make me doubt the contributions in the paper. 

- 2.1, The author should provide more baselines (Adam, SGD with momentum) so that we can really have a fair evaluation. 

- 2.2, For Figure 1, my understanding is that the authors are trying to show ISAAC still obtains good results (loss and accuracy) even with large regularization. If so, I do not think it is necessary. As the authors stated in Theorem 1, larger regularization makes ISAAC behave more like SGD. Therefore, getting similar loss and accuracy is no surprise.
    
- 2.3, For Figure 2, there are several issues. First, hyper parameters are not fully revealed. For instance, what is the weight decay for each optimizer since weight decays large affects training convergence?  Second, only the training curve is reported. The author really should present both a training and validation curve. In many cases, we can have faster convergence on training dataset, but not for validation dataset. 

- 2.4, For Figure 6, the reported accuracy is obviously lower compared to results we usually have on CIFAR-10. The reason might be that the authors use SGD without momentum. However, I am not convinced under such a setting. SGD with momentum is a default setting in many training tasks. Without momentum, I do not think the results will provide meaningful comparisons and conclusions.

3, Training with small batches should be further justified. I understand the authors stress the compute benefits of ISAAC in a small-batch regime (Theorem 3-ii). However, considering more general settings, the authors should well justify it: is it worth accelerating the training using small batches given the fact that large batches can also improve convergence performance even using SGD. Therefore, I think not only should the author provide results in small-batch regimes, training using large batches should also be evaluated and compared with other baselines.

Questions:
See my comments above.

Limitations:
No

extracted_ideas.summary: The review recognizes the paper as clearly written and technically sound but argues that its core contribution is limited, primarily because the proposed regularization scheme tends to reduce to SGD behavior. It also criticizes the empirical evaluation for lacking strong baselines, missing details, and insufficient justification of the small-batch focus.
strengths: Well-written manuscript with clearly explained preliminaries and methods.
Provides a theoretical guarantee that the update direction remains correct under the proposed adaptation.
weaknesses: Perceived limited novelty: separating regularization terms and using large regularization appears to make the method behave like SGD.
Theorem 1 implies the method interpolates between K-FAC (small lambdas) and vanilla SGD (large lambdas), undermining the novelty claim.
Empirical evidence is not strong enough to support the claim that ISAAC behaves like K-FAC under strong regularization.
Insufficient baselines in experiments (e.g., missing Adam and SGD with momentum).
Figure 1’s conclusion is unsurprising given Theorem 1 (large regularization ≈ SGD), making the result less informative.
Figure 2 lacks full hyperparameter disclosure (e.g., weight decay) and reports only training curves without validation curves.
Figure 6 uses SGD without momentum, leading to unusually low CIFAR-10 accuracy and limiting the meaningfulness of comparisons.
Small-batch emphasis is insufficiently justified; lacks evaluation under large-batch settings where SGD can also perform well.
suggestions: Include additional baselines such as Adam and SGD with momentum for fair comparisons.
Report full hyperparameter configurations, particularly weight decay settings for each optimizer.
Provide both training and validation (or test) curves to assess generalization, not just training convergence.
Re-evaluate experiments like CIFAR-10 with standard settings (e.g., SGD with momentum) to ensure meaningful comparisons.
Strengthen empirical evidence demonstrating that ISAAC retains K-FAC-like benefits beyond what would be expected from SGD behavior at large regularization.
Justify the focus on small-batch regimes and also evaluate performance with large batches, comparing against strong baselines.
main_points: Core methodological novelty is questioned because the approach effectively interpolates between K-FAC and SGD, and large regularization leads to SGD-like behavior.
Empirical evaluation lacks strong baselines and critical details, weakening claims about performance and efficiency.
Some experimental results are expected or uninformative given the theoretical mapping (e.g., large regularization behaving like SGD).
Experimental setups (e.g., CIFAR-10 without momentum) reduce the relevance of comparisons.
The focus on small-batch benefits needs broader justification, including results with larger batches.
technical_details: Two Tikhonov regularization terms (λ_g and λ_a) are introduced; Theorem 1 states small λ_g, λ_a approximate K-FAC while large λ_g, λ_a approximate vanilla SGD.
Efficiency claim relies on forcing λ_g → ∞ to avoid computing expensive curvature terms.
Empirical baselines are missing key optimizers (Adam, SGD with momentum) necessary for fair evaluation.
Figure 2 lacks disclosure of weight decay settings, which materially affect convergence.
Figure 2 reports only training curves; validation/test curves are needed to assess generalization.
Figure 6 uses SGD without momentum on CIFAR-10, contributing to lower-than-typical accuracy.
Theorem 3-ii emphasizes computational benefits in small-batch regimes; reviewer asks for evaluation in large-batch scenarios as well.

reviews.summary:
In unconstrained optimisation, Newton's method uses inverse Hessian to determine the step size of the update. When applied to deep learning, the sheer size of the Hessian matrix makes Newton's method intractable. Among existing attempts to address this challenge, K-FAC is a promising approach. In practice, Tikhonov regularization/damping is used to avoid divergence.

In this paper, the authors introduced an extension to the standard Tikhonov damping for K-FAC. The main idea is to use two independent damping parameters (hyper-parameters for regularisation) instead of one damping parameter, one for the gradient factor and one for the activity factor, as shown in Equation (17). The authors explored special cases where the two parameters are either 0 or infinity. They also discussed the special case of setting one parameter to infinity, effectively removing the gradient factor in K-FAC. One major claim was that this activity-only form has an advantage in reduced computational complexity, although I don't find a theoretical comparison with K-FAC in this aspect. 

The claim for computational efficiency was mostly supported via empirical evaluation.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:

1. The paper addresses an important problem: efficient approximation of Gaussian-Newton approximation
2. The exposition is mostly clear, especially if you are already familiar with the existing literature.
3. The experiments seem comprehensive.

Weaknesses:

1. It is not clear what is the main contribution of this paper. Is it the two-parameter damping mechanism (What advantage do we gain)? Or is it the special case mentioned in Remark 3 (Why this is a still good approximation without $g^{\top}g$)? 
2. Many theoretical results presented look rather trivial.
reviews.questions: What is the intuition behind Eq (21)? Compared with Eq (13), why is it a good approximation?

Summary:
In unconstrained optimisation, Newton's method uses inverse Hessian to determine the step size of the update. When applied to deep learning, the sheer size of the Hessian matrix makes Newton's method intractable. Among existing attempts to address this challenge, K-FAC is a promising approach. In practice, Tikhonov regularization/damping is used to avoid divergence.

In this paper, the authors introduced an extension to the standard Tikhonov damping for K-FAC. The main idea is to use two independent damping parameters (hyper-parameters for regularisation) instead of one damping parameter, one for the gradient factor and one for the activity factor, as shown in Equation (17). The authors explored special cases where the two parameters are either 0 or infinity. They also discussed the special case of setting one parameter to infinity, effectively removing the gradient factor in K-FAC. One major claim was that this activity-only form has an advantage in reduced computational complexity, although I don't find a theoretical comparison with K-FAC in this aspect. 

The claim for computational efficiency was mostly supported via empirical evaluation.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:

1. The paper addresses an important problem: efficient approximation of Gaussian-Newton approximation
2. The exposition is mostly clear, especially if you are already familiar with the existing literature.
3. The experiments seem comprehensive.

Weaknesses:

1. It is not clear what is the main contribution of this paper. Is it the two-parameter damping mechanism (What advantage do we gain)? Or is it the special case mentioned in Remark 3 (Why this is a still good approximation without $g^{\top}g$)? 
2. Many theoretical results presented look rather trivial.

Questions:
What is the intuition behind Eq (21)? Compared with Eq (13), why is it a good approximation?

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
Summary
This paper targets an important goal: making second-order optimization, particularly Gauss-Newton/K-FAC, more efficient for deep learning. The authors extend standard Tikhonov damping in K-FAC to use two independent regularization parameters for the gradient and activity factors (Eq. 17). They also highlight a special case obtained by taking one parameter to infinity (Eq. 21), which removes the gradient factor and yields an activity-only conditioner that is claimed to be computationally cheaper than the standard K-FAC factorization (Eq. 13). The exposition is generally clear—especially for readers familiar with the K-FAC literature—and the experimental evaluation appears comprehensive.

Strengths
- The paper addresses a timely and important problem: efficient approximation of Gauss-Newton/K-FAC for deep networks.
- The presentation is mostly clear, and the paper is easy to follow for those conversant with prior work.
- The empirical coverage is broad and helps substantiate the practical relevance of the proposed variants.

Weaknesses
- The central novelty is not crisply articulated. It remains ambiguous whether the principal contribution is the introduction of two-parameter damping (with its benefits), or the activity-only special case that omits the gradient factor.
- Many of the theoretical results come across as straightforward and do not significantly deepen understanding beyond known formulations.
- The claimed computational efficiency of the activity-only variant is supported primarily by empirical results; there is no formal theoretical comparison of computational complexity vis-à-vis standard K-FAC.
- The paper does not provide sufficient intuition for why the activity-only update (Eq. 21) should be a good approximation to the K-FAC form (Eq. 13).

Suggestions for improvement
- Clearly state the primary contribution and its concrete advantages. If the two-parameter damping is the main advance, spell out how it improves over standard damping (e.g., stability, conditioning, or convergence behavior). If the activity-only special case is the focus, explain its distinct benefits and scope.
- Provide a theoretical comparison of computational complexity between the activity-only approach and standard K-FAC. Even a high-level analysis would help substantiate the efficiency claims that are currently demonstrated mostly empirically.
- Offer intuition for Eq. (21): explain the circumstances under which discarding the gradient factor is reasonable, and how this relates to the structure of the Kronecker factors in Eq. (13). A clear rationale for when and why the approximation holds would greatly strengthen the case.
- Where possible, strengthen the theoretical contributions beyond results that are essentially immediate from existing formulations.

Overall assessment and recommendation
The paper is well written and empirically solid, and it tackles an important practical problem in second-order optimization. However, the contribution would benefit from clearer positioning of the main novelty, stronger theoretical support for the activity-only variant’s efficiency, and more intuition justifying the key approximation. On balance, I lean toward a borderline accept: the importance of the problem and the comprehensive experiments outweigh the reservations about theoretical depth and contribution clarity, but addressing the above points would significantly improve the paper. Rating: 5 (Borderline accept).
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
In unconstrained optimisation, Newton's method uses inverse Hessian to determine the step size of the update. When applied to deep learning, the sheer size of the Hessian matrix makes Newton's method intractable. Among existing attempts to address this challenge, K-FAC is a promising approach. In practice, Tikhonov regularization/damping is used to avoid divergence.

In this paper, the authors introduced an extension to the standard Tikhonov damping for K-FAC. The main idea is to use two independent damping parameters (hyper-parameters for regularisation) instead of one damping parameter, one for the gradient factor and one for the activity factor, as shown in Equation (17). The authors explored special cases where the two parameters are either 0 or infinity. They also discussed the special case of setting one parameter to infinity, effectively removing the gradient factor in K-FAC. One major claim was that this activity-only form has an advantage in reduced computational complexity, although I don't find a theoretical comparison with K-FAC in this aspect. 

The claim for computational efficiency was mostly supported via empirical evaluation.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:

1. The paper addresses an important problem: efficient approximation of Gaussian-Newton approximation
2. The exposition is mostly clear, especially if you are already familiar with the existing literature.
3. The experiments seem comprehensive.

Weaknesses:

1. It is not clear what is the main contribution of this paper. Is it the two-parameter damping mechanism (What advantage do we gain)? Or is it the special case mentioned in Remark 3 (Why this is a still good approximation without $g^{\top}g$)? 
2. Many theoretical results presented look rather trivial.

Questions:
What is the intuition behind Eq (21)? Compared with Eq (13), why is it a good approximation?

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

extracted_ideas.summary: The review finds the paper addresses an important problem with generally clear exposition and comprehensive experiments, but questions the clarity of the main contribution and the depth of the theoretical advances. It also notes a lack of theoretical comparison for the claimed computational efficiency of the activity-only variant and asks for intuition behind a key approximation.
strengths: Addresses an important problem: efficient approximation of Gauss-Newton (and K-FAC) for deep learning
Exposition is mostly clear (especially for readers familiar with the literature)
Experiments appear comprehensive
weaknesses: Unclear main contribution: whether the key novelty is the two-parameter damping mechanism (and its advantages) or the activity-only special case (removing the gradient factor)
Many theoretical results seem trivial
No theoretical comparison of computational complexity between the activity-only variant and K-FAC; efficiency is supported mostly by empirical results
Insufficient intuition and justification for why the activity-only update (Eq. 21) is a good approximation compared to the K-FAC form (Eq. 13)
suggestions: Clarify the primary contribution and its concrete advantages (two-parameter damping vs. the activity-only special case)
Provide a theoretical comparison of computational complexity between the proposed activity-only approach and K-FAC
Explain the intuition behind Eq. (21) and justify why it is a good approximation relative to Eq. (13)
Strengthen the theoretical results beyond those that appear trivial
main_points: The paper extends standard Tikhonov damping in K-FAC to two independent damping parameters (for gradient and activity factors) as in Eq. (17)
A key special case sets one parameter to infinity (Eq. 21), effectively removing the gradient factor and claiming reduced computational complexity
The claim of computational efficiency for the activity-only form lacks theoretical comparison to K-FAC and is mainly supported empirically
Theoretical contributions are perceived as largely trivial
Overall clarity and experimental coverage are good, but the central novelty and its benefits need clearer articulation
technical_details: Two independent damping (regularization) parameters are introduced for the gradient factor and the activity factor in the K-FAC framework (Eq. 17)
Special case with one parameter set to infinity yields an activity-only conditioner that omits the gradient factor (Eq. 21)
Comparison point is the standard K-FAC Kronecker factorization (Eq. 13)
Context relies on Tikhonov regularization/damping to stabilize second-order methods
Computational efficiency claims for the activity-only variant lack a formal theoretical comparison against K-FAC; support is primarily empirical

reviews.summary: Inspired by the KFAC approximation and the Tikhonov regularization of the Newton method, this paper proposes a regularized K-FAC approximated Gauss-Newton type method called ISAAC. reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:

Regularization parameters is crucial for second-order type methods.  The authors first approximate the GGN matrix as a kroncker product of two matrices, and then view two regulation parameters independently. By letting the two regularization parameters vanish or go to infinity, one can obtain new directions and therefore new methods. The proposed method is more efficient than KFAC since only one matrix should be inverted and no need extra propagation to compute $g$.  

Weaknesses:

This paper should be polished to make it easier to read. 

Some related literatures on second-order methods should be mentioned, such as Shampoo[1], KBFGS[2], NG+[3] and SENG[4]. 

Some theoretical properties is natural and trivial. It is better to formalize an algorithm and give a convergence result.

It is better to add some large numerical examples, such as Resnet50 with Imagenet-1k and make a comparison with other second-order methods Shampoo[1], KBFGS[2], NG+[3] and SENG[4]. 

[1] Gupta,V.,Koren,T., Singer,Y.:, Shampoo: Preconditioned stochastic tensor optimization. In: International Conference on Machine Learning.

[2] Goldfarb, D., Ren, Y., Bahamou, A.:, Practical quasi-Newton methods for training deep neural networks. In:Advances inNeural Information Processing Systems.

[3] Yang, M., Xu, D., Cui, Q., Wen, Z., & Xu, P. (2021). NG+: A Multi-Step Matrix-Product Natural Gradient Method for Deep Learning. arXiv preprint arXiv:2106.07454.

[4] Yang, M., Xu, D., Wen, Z., Chen, M., & Xu, P. (2020). Sketch-based empirical natural gradient methods for deep learning. Journal of Scientific Computing.
reviews.questions:
line 60: change [14],[15] to [14.15]. It is better to put multiple references in the same brackets. Please check other places.

It is better to add the discussion between GGN matrix and Fisher matrix, which can refer to [1,2] and related literatures.

It is better to add a left brackets after $\eta$ and a right bracket before the gradient in eq.(16). Please also add brackets in a suitable place in eq. (17).

In eq.(19), the gradient should be $\mathrm{g}^\top a /b$ where the $b$ is missed. In eq.(19), the authors use the same batch to approximate the GGN matrix and compute the gradient. Can the batches for approximation of the GGN and the computation of the gradient be different? In that case, what the related theoretical properties will be?

When the objective function is convex, the GGN matrix is positive semi-definite matrix. Adding a regularization parameter in GGN, It is t easy to show that $\zeta$ is an ascent direction, which means Theorem 2 is trivial.

The authors consider the fully-connected neural network in this paper. I believe this method can be extended to convolutional neural networks. And in numerical part, the authors have done some experiments. I wonder can we obtain some explanation from theory for this case? 

For the kronecker approximation of the GGN matrix, $\mathrm{a}\mathrm{a}^\top$ is more important than $\overline{g}^\top\overline{g}$ part. I once validate it numerically. One reason is that each value of the matrix $\overline{g}^\top\overline{g}$  is very small so that the $\lambda_g$ domains. I have noticed one related work recently. The work is named NG+[3].

[1] Botev, Aleksandar, Hippolyt Ritter, and David Barber. "Practical gauss-newton optimisation for deep learning." International Conference on Machine Learning. PMLR, 2017.

[2] Martens, James. "New insights and perspectives on the natural gradient method." The Journal of Machine Learning Research 21.1 (2020): 5776-5851.

[3] Yang, M., Xu, D., Cui, Q., Wen, Z., & Xu, P. (2021). NG+: A Multi-Step Matrix-Product Natural Gradient Method for Deep Learning. arXiv preprint arXiv:2106.07454.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
Inspired by the KFAC approximation and the Tikhonov regularization of the Newton method, this paper proposes a regularized K-FAC approximated Gauss-Newton type method called ISAAC. The proposed method is based on only the input to a respective layer, therefore does not suffer from a substantial computational overhead. Numerical experiments show the advantages of the proposed method compared to both first-order type and second-order type methods.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:

The proposed method is more efficient than KFAC since only one matrix should be inverted and no need extra propagation to compute $g$.  

Weaknesses:

This paper should be polished to make it easier to read.

Some theoretical properties is natural and trivial. It is better to formalize an algorithm and give a convergence result.

It is better to add some large numerical examples, such as Resnet50 with Imagenet-1k and make a comparison with other second-order methods Shampoo[1], KBFGS[2], NG+[3] and SENG[4].

[2] Goldfarb, D., Ren, Y., Bahamou, A.:, Practical quasi-Newton methods for training deep neural networks. In:Advances inNeural Information Processing Systems.

[3] Yang, M., Xu, D., Cui, Q., Wen, Z., & Xu, P. (2021). NG+: A Multi-Step Matrix-Product Natural Gradient Method for Deep Learning. arXiv preprint arXiv:2106.07454.

[4] Yang, M., Xu, D., Wen, Z., Chen, M., & Xu, P. (2020). Sketch-based empirical natural gradient methods for deep learning. Journal of Scientific Computing.

Response:

title: LeRaC: Learning Rate Curriculum

reviews.summary: This paper proposes to modify neural net training by applying a different learning rate to each layer: the deeper layers are assigned a lower learning rate that progressively grows to match the first layer learning rate.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
The paper is clear, the approach is simple and can be implemented easily. The experiments shows better generalization when adopting the proposed learning rate schedule. The paper could be improved on two main axes:
(i) presenting the approach as an optimization method seem more appropriate than the curriculum learning angle, (ii) more analysis and justifications of why the method works seem necessary.
Overall, I feel that the paper does not explain how/if the proposed differentiated learning rate schedule is different from the effective learning rates reached by different settings of adaptive optimizers (momentum, Adadelta, Adam, SM3...).
reviews.questions:
== Optimization vs curriculum learning ==

The proposed method uses a different learning rate per layer. This endeavor has been widely explored in the literature on optimization of neural nets while the name curriculum learning is usually employed for methods modifying the ordering of the examples during training. I agree that the name curriculum could make sense in your context but the paper needs to spend more time on previous work on optimization. This means expanding the related work section and the empirical analysis.

The Adadelta paper (Zeiler 2012) observes the benefit to use different learning rates for different layers and Adadelta results in "effective learning rates [...] are larger for the lower layers of the network and much
smaller for the top layer at the beginning of training." Similar observations can be made for other adaptive learning rate algorithms like Adagrad, Adam, Adafactor, Adamax and AdamW or even later algorithms like e.g. SM3 (Memory-Efficient Adaptive Optimization, NeurIPS'19, see Figure 1.).

To justify your approach it seems necessary to compare your method at least with Adam and report the effective average learning rate (e.g. see Zeiler 2012's definition) per layer for both methods and how their parameters control the differentiation of learning rates across layers during training. The fact that your method is used in conjunction with Adam (or variants) could mean that either (i) the same type of learning rate schedule could be attained by changing Adam's parameters or (ii) your method's benefit comes from schedules unreachable by Adam. It seems very important to explore both methods' parameters to distinguish these two cases.

== Benefit is faster training or better generalization? ==

Modifying the training algorithm requires reporting training curves with *both* validation and training loss over time. The paper does not report any training loss which left us wonder if the generalization benefit comes from better optimization (lower training and validation loss) versus a better generalization trajectory (reaching a lower validation loss at the same training loss). 

== Schedule Tuning ==

Could you explain how the exponential scheduler of Eq. 8 has been selected? Is it the only option that you tried? How is the training speed affected by the choice of the additional hyperparameters (c, \nu^{(0)}_n, k)? In particular, compared to a robust baseline like Adam, would most hyperparameter choices yield faster or slower training? I do not understand how c can be set to 10 without validation.

== Number of training steps ==

The experimental section should spend more time describing how the number of training steps was selected. In which cases the baselines would reach the same or better validation loss with more steps? E.g. Figure 1.d. seems to suggest that better validation loss with CBS can be reached with more training. This improvement does not seem to agree with the sentence "all models are trained with early stopping", does it?

== Justification of the noise argument ==

Adaptive methods (Adam etc) justify higher learning rates for first layers with a curvature argument: in laymans term, to get the same impact on the output the first layers' updates require a larger learning rates than the last ones. Adadelta (Zeiler 2012) also mentions the larger learning rate in light of the vanishing gradient effect (Bengio 2014). Your motivation (from [21]) is to reduce the effect of "noise due to untrained parameters". This argument is unclear to the reader and it would help if you could find a way to measure/characterize that "noise" and show how your method reduces its effect.
reviews.limitations: This work does not raises concerns on societal impact.
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

Summary:
This paper proposes to modify neural net training by applying a different learning rate to each layer: the deeper layers are assigned a lower learning rate that progressively grows to match the first layer learning rate.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
The paper is clear, the approach is simple and can be implemented easily. The experiments shows better generalization when adopting the proposed learning rate schedule. The paper could be improved on two main axes:
(i) presenting the approach as an optimization method seem more appropriate than the curriculum learning angle, (ii) more analysis and justifications of why the method works seem necessary.
Overall, I feel that the paper does not explain how/if the proposed differentiated learning rate schedule is different from the effective learning rates reached by different settings of adaptive optimizers (momentum, Adadelta, Adam, SM3...).

Questions:
== Optimization vs curriculum learning ==

The proposed method uses a different learning rate per layer. I agree that the name curriculum could make sense in your context but the paper needs to spend more time on previous work on optimization. This means expanding the related work section and the empirical analysis.

The Adadelta paper (Zeiler 2012) observes the benefit to use different learning rates for different layers and Adadelta results in "effective learning rates [...] are larger for the lower layers of the network and much
smaller for the top layer at the beginning of training." Similar observations can be made for other adaptive learning rate algorithms like Adagrad, Adam, Adafactor, Adamax and AdamW or even later algorithms like e.g. SM3 (Memory-Efficient Adaptive Optimization, NeurIPS'19, see Figure 1.).
